entries.title: Troubleshooting: section overview
entries.body:
# Troubleshooting - overview

When Burp does not behave the way you expect - it won't start, it lags, it does not intercept traffic - the documentation has a dedicated troubleshooting section. It covers four areas:

- **Common errors** - typical startup, proxy, certificate, network errors and what to do about them.
- **Performance** - what to do about slowdowns, memory and heavy projects/scans.
- **Launch from command line** - how to launch Burp from the terminal and why (memory management, headless).
- **Setting Java options** - how to set JVM options (heap size, RAM share) so that they survive an update.

## The first diagnostic tool

Whatever breaks, start with **Dashboard > Event log**. Burp writes task starts, warnings and errors there - connection failures, TLS problems, going out of scope, running out of memory. Very often the cause is visible there right away, and there is no need to guess.

```
Something is wrong -> Dashboard > Event log -> read Critical/Error
Won't start        -> launch from the command line -> see the error text
Lags               -> Settings > Suite > Performance + disk project
```

> Tip: if Burp won't start at all and there is no window, launch it from the command line (`java -jar ...`). Then the error message that you would not see in the GUI is printed to the terminal - and it immediately becomes clear what the problem is (Java version, path to the jar, lack of memory).

Next - each topic in detail.

entries.title: Common errors and their solutions
entries.body:
# Common errors and their solutions

The most frequent Burp problems and proven fixes.

## Burp won't start

- **`NoClassDefFoundError` on startup.** You are running the jar incorrectly. Make sure you use the `-jar` argument with the CORRECT path to the jar file. Check the version: you need **Java 21** or newer (`java -version`). If necessary, specify the absolute path to the required `java`. If the Java installation is broken - reinstall it.
- **Burp silently fails to start.** Launch it from the command line - then you will see the error text that is hidden in the GUI, and you will understand the cause.
- **The JAR "unpacked" instead of launching.** Do not unpack the jar as an archive. Fix the `.jar` file association to Java, or launch from the command line.

## Browser and proxy

- **The browser cannot send requests (through Burp).** In order: turn off interception in **Proxy > Intercept**; try a different domain; check that the proxy listener is running (**Settings > Tools > Proxy**); compare the proxy settings in the browser with Burp (by default `127.0.0.1:8080`); check the upstream proxy settings; look at the **Event log**; as a last resort, restore settings to defaults and restart Burp.
- **Burp does not intercept.** Check whether traffic is flowing into **Proxy > HTTP history**; enable interception and restore the default interception rules; make sure the browser proxies BOTH HTTP AND HTTPS with no exceptions.
- **HTTPS specifically is not intercepted.** Configure the browser to proxy both protocols - both HTTP and HTTPS.
- **HTTPS sites break / security warnings.** Install the **Burp CA** in the browser (or use the built-in **Burp's browser**, where the certificate is already trusted).

## Authentication and network

- **Authentication failures (platform).** Configure platform authentication in **Settings > Network > Connections** - add entries for the required hosts with credentials.
- **Mobile device traffic does not arrive.** Check that the firewall on the PC allows incoming connections from the device, and that the device is configured per the instructions in the mobile section.

## Scaling on 4K (Windows)

If on a 4K screen the interface looks tiny/distorted, launch Burp with the flags that disable hardware-accelerated rendering:

```
start javaw -Dsun.java2d.d3d=false -Dsun.java2d.noddraw=true -jar burpsuite_pro_vXXXX.X.jar
```

> Tip: the lion's share of "the proxy doesn't work" is either **Intercept** that is enabled and forgotten (traffic is queued and does not go through), or a mismatch of proxy settings between the browser and Burp. Check these two things first.

> Pitfall: with startup errors Java is almost always to blame - the wrong version (<21), the wrong `java` in PATH, or launching without `-jar`. The `java -version` command and an explicit path to the jar solve most such cases.

entries.title: Performance
entries.body:
# Performance

If Burp lags, eats all the memory or hangs on large scans - there are three directions you can tune: memory, CPU and network.

## Memory

- **Disable unnecessary extensions.** PortSwigger does not optimize third-party extensions for resource usage - a greedy extension easily eats up memory. Disable them one by one to find the culprit.
- **Raise/lower the memory limit.** **Settings > Suite > Performance > Maximum memory usage** - set your own value. The minimum is 256 MB. For large projects, if free RAM is available, increase it; to free up resources for the system - decrease it. Changes are applied after RESTARTING Burp.
- **Use a disk project.** A temporary project (in memory) eats more memory than a disk one. Switch via **Project > Save copy**. Keep the file on a local disk, not a network one.

## System requirements

Burp requires 64-bit hardware. For Professional, PortSwigger recommends at least 8 GB RAM and 2 CPU cores; for large/complex applications you need a more powerful machine.

## CPU

- **Disable pretty print.** **Settings > UI > Message editor** -> uncheck **Pretty print by default**. Otherwise, on large files (JS, bulky responses) Burp spends CPU on formatting every time you open them.
- **Disable JavaScript analysis in the scan.** Take the ready-made config **Audit checks - all except JavaScript analysis**, or disable it manually: **Burp > Configuration library > Auditing > Scan checks > JavaScript**. JS analysis is one of the heaviest checks.
- **Tune scan performance.** Ready-made configs: **Crawl strategy - fastest**, crawl limits (10/30/60 minutes), **Audit checks - light/medium active**. Or manually: reduce crawl depth and time, disable intrusive checks, set the audit speed to **Fast**.
- **Narrow the scan scope.** Through **Detailed scope configuration** in the scan launch wizard, limit scanning to only the areas of interest.
- **Specify only the protocols you need.** In the scan details, leave only HTTP or only HTTPS, so as not to send unnecessary requests.

## Network

- **Fewer simultaneous scans.** Parallel scans add up into load on the network and on the target.
- **Resource pools.** Next to **New Live Task** there is a settings icon - that is where you create/edit resource pools. By reducing the maximum number of concurrent tasks in a pool, you lower the load on the target and remove timeouts from overload.

```
Memory: Settings > Suite > Performance > Maximum memory usage (restart)
        + disk project (Project > Save copy)
CPU:    uncheck Pretty print; config without JavaScript analysis; Audit speed Fast
Network: fewer parallel scans; Resource pools -> task limit
```

> Tip: the main cause of slowdowns during hunting is a bloated scope and a heavy audit on a giant application. A narrow scope + a fast scan profile give more gain than any amount of tweaking memory.

> Pitfall: cranking **Maximum memory usage** up to the ceiling of physical RAM is a bad idea. If you allocate almost all the memory to Burp, nothing is left for the system and the built-in Chromium, and everything starts swapping and lagging even worse. Leave headroom for the OS.

entries.title: Launch from the command line
entries.body:
# Launching Burp from the command line

Launching from the terminal is needed when you have to control Burp's behavior at startup - first of all the amount of memory - and also to see startup errors or run Burp without graphics (headless).

## Basic syntax

```
java -jar -Xmx4g /path/to/burp.jar
```

- `-jar /path/to/burp.jar` - the actual Burp jar;
- `-Xmx4g` - allocate 4 GB to the heap. Adjust to your RAM.

## Java requirements

You need **Java 21** or newer. Check:

```
java -version
```

If there is no Java or the version is old - install a fresh JRE. Note: the NATIVE Burp installers come with their own bundled Java, but when launching via `java -jar` you use YOUR own Java installation and are responsible for updating it yourself.

## Useful arguments

| Argument | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `--help` | show the available options |
| `--disable-extensions` | do not load extensions at startup |
| `--project-file=<file>` | open/create the specified project |
| `--config-file=<file>` | load a project config |
| `-Djava.awt.headless=true` | headless mode (no GUI) |
| `-Xmx4g` | maximum heap 4 GB |
| `-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=50` | limit memory to 50% of the system's |

```
# increase memory and open a specific project
java -jar -Xmx8g /path/to/burp.jar --project-file=/burp/acme.burp

# recover a crashed Burp without extensions
java -jar /path/to/burp.jar --disable-extensions
```

> Tip: `--disable-extensions` is a lifeline when Burp crashes or hangs at startup because of a broken extension. Started up without extensions -> removed the culprit -> launched normally.

> Pitfall: the path to the jar and the case of the file name must be exact (the jar is named something like `burpsuite_pro_vXXXX.X.jar`). Launching without `-jar`, an incorrect path, or old Java (<21) are the three main reasons why a command-line launch does not work.

entries.title: Setting Java options (vmoptions)
entries.body:
# Setting Java options

If you installed Burp with the native installer (rather than launching via `java -jar`), you can set JVM options - heap size, RAM share - through the `.vmoptions` file. The correct way is NOT to touch the default file, but to create a `user.vmoptions` next to it: it survives Burp updates, whereas the default one is overwritten on upgrade.

## Where the file is located

- **Windows / Linux** - in the Burp installation folder.
- **macOS** - right-click Burp Suite -> **Show Package Contents** -> the **Contents** folder.

Names of the default files:

- **Windows / Linux**: `BurpSuitePro.vmoptions` or `BurpSuiteCommunity.vmoptions`;
- **macOS**: `vmoptions.txt`.

## How to create user.vmoptions

1. Find the default `vmoptions` file in the appropriate folder.
2. Copy it.
3. Rename the copy to `user.vmoptions`.

## Syntax

Each option is on its own line:

```
-Xmx4g
-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=50
```

- `-Xmx4g` - maximum heap 4 GB;
- `-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=50` - give Burp up to 50% of system memory.

## Limitation

Through `user.vmoptions` you set ONLY standard Java virtual machine options (heap size, RAM usage). Burp's own settings are not set through this file - for those there are Settings and config files.

```
# user.vmoptions (next to the default one, survives upgrade)
-Xmx8g
```

> Tip: `user.vmoptions` is the correct way to permanently raise the memory for a native installation. Unlike a manual `java -jar -Xmx...`, you do not have to repeat it on every launch, and it will not be lost after a Burp update.

> Pitfall: do not edit the DEFAULT `.vmoptions` directly - the next Burp update will overwrite it, and your edits will disappear. Only `user.vmoptions`. And remember the limit: do not allocate almost all the RAM to Burp, leave headroom for the OS and the built-in browser.
